Case Study · 2022

Equity-Centered Design
Research —
Informal Food Systems

Why does sustainable packaging keep failing street food vendors in India? I went to find out — 50+ vendor sites, material samples, chemical migration tests, and a supply chain no one had mapped before.

Role
Design Researcher
Organization
BLG Group
Year
2022
Focus
Ethnographic Research · HCD · Equity Design

The informal food economy — millions of vendors, daily transactions, zero design attention.

The Context

A design problem hiding in plain sight

Street food in India feeds hundreds of millions of people every day. It's one of the largest informal economies in the world. And it runs almost entirely on packaging that was never designed for the job — printed newspaper, plastic bags, styrofoam, repurposed containers.

Sustainable packaging solutions exist. They're designed, tested, and marketed. They keep failing in this context. The question I wanted to answer was: why?

This was a self-initiated research project. No client, no brief. Just a problem I kept seeing and couldn't stop thinking about.

"Sustainable design keeps failing here — not because vendors don't care, but because every solution ignores the economics."
50+
Vendor sites visited
across urban India
₹0.13
Maximum cost per plate
any intervention must meet
6
Packaging types
collected & tested
0
Existing solutions that
met the price constraint
The Process

How I approached it

01 — OBSERVE
Field Visits
50+ vendor sites across Hyderabad and Bengaluru. Watching how food gets packaged, handed over, and consumed in real time.
02 — COLLECT
Material Sampling
Collected every type of packaging in use — newspaper, plastic, thermocol, leaf plates, aluminum foil, thermocol containers.
03 — TEST
Chemical Migration
Tested printed newspaper packaging for ink migration under heat and moisture — the exact conditions of serving hot food.
04 — MAP
Supply Chain
Traced packaging from manufacturer to distributor to vendor — mapping cost at every step to understand where the ₹0.13 constraint comes from.

Observation board from field collection: newspaper cones, thermocol plates, leaf plates, aluminum containers — each annotated with use case and failure mode. "Jugaad" vs "Formalizing" — two ends of the spectrum.

What I Found

The field told a different story

Paper is the dominant medium — but not by design. Vendors use printed newspaper because it's free, flexible, and available everywhere. It folds into cones for mixtures, wraps samosas, lines plates. It's an accidental design system that works perfectly within its constraints.

The problem is the ink. Printed newspaper contains heavy metal-based inks and solvents. Under heat and moisture — exactly the conditions of serving hot bhaji or greasy samosas — these migrate directly into the food. There's no food-safe barrier.

But here's what sustainable design interventions miss: the newspaper costs nothing. A vendor serving 200 customers a day at ₹10–15 per item cannot absorb even a ₹0.50 increase in packaging cost. Every "sustainable" alternative I found cost more than the vendor's entire per-unit margin on packaging.

Field visit — a coconut and snack vendor in Bengaluru. Typical setup: tarpaulin shade, open cart, high footfall, no fixed infrastructure.

Packaging in use across 50+ sites — newspaper cones, thermocol cups, plastic bags, leaf plates. Each tells a story about cost, availability, and improvisation.

Key Findings

Three things no one was saying

01
The economics are non-negotiable
Packaging cost sits at ₹0.10–0.13 per serve. Any intervention above this price point will be rejected — not because vendors don't care about safety, but because the margin doesn't exist. This is a design constraint, not a values problem.
02
Newspaper is a design system
Vendors have invented a sophisticated informal packaging system using newspaper — cones, wraps, liners, plates. Any intervention needs to match this functional versatility. A plate that only works as a plate will fail.
03
The supply chain is the design opportunity
Packaging doesn't reach vendors through design channels — it comes through local distributors and waste paper markets. An intervention that enters through this existing supply chain has a real chance. One that doesn't, doesn't.
"The newspaper is not the problem. The ink is. And the ink is there because no one designed an alternative at the right price point."
The Intervention

Designing within the constraint

The design brief I set for myself: a packaging intervention at ₹0.10–0.13 per unit, manufacturable without industrial equipment, distributable through existing waste paper channels, and functionally equivalent to newspaper — meaning it needed to fold, wrap, and hold like newspaper does.

The solution I developed was a food-safe kraft paper sheet sized to replicate the most common newspaper formats used in street food — designed for production by women's self-help groups (SHGs) using manual cutting and rolling equipment already available in most SHG setups.

The SHG production model wasn't just an ethical choice — it was an economic one. SHG labor costs are competitive with the price of waste newspaper. It's the only production model that can match the ₹0.13 constraint.

What I Learned

What this project changed in how I design

Economic constraints are design constraints. I used to think of cost as something that came after design. This project taught me that cost is the first design decision. If your intervention isn't viable at the price point of the people it's for, it isn't a design — it's a prototype that will never be used.

Informal systems are not broken systems. The newspaper packaging system is ingenious. It's flexible, free, zero-waste in acquisition, and perfectly adapted to its context. Design research that starts by calling it a problem will miss everything that makes it work.

Field research changes what you think the problem is. I went in thinking this was a materials problem. I came out knowing it was a supply chain and economics problem. That shift only happened because I was physically present — watching, collecting, asking.
